Software & datasets

Research

Below are the main directions of current and ongoing work in the lab.

Past work

Groundwater and landscape coevolution

My foundational work developed coupled groundwater–landscape evolution models to understand how subsurface properties and climate are expressed in topography, and how landscapes respond to tectonics.

Publications

Litwin, D. G., & Harman, C. J. (2024). Evidence of Subsurface Control on the Coevolution of Hillslope Morphology and Runoff Generation. Water Resources Research, https://doi.org/10.1029/2024WR037301

Litwin, D. G., Tucker, G., Barnhart, K., & Harman, C. (2020). GroundwaterDupuitPercolator: A Landlab component for groundwater flow. Journal of Open Source Software, https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.01935

Litwin, D. G., Tucker, G. E., Barnhart, K. R., & Harman, C. J. (2022). Groundwater affects the geomorphic and hydrologic properties of coevolved landscapes. Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface, https://doi.org/10.1029/2021JF006239

Litwin, D. G., Tucker, G. E., Barnhart, K. R., & Harman, C. J. (2024). Catchment coevolution and the geomorphic origins of variable source area hydrology. Water Resources Research, https://doi.org/10.1029/2023WR034647

Ongoing

Channel-hillslope coupling

I work on improving how landscape evolution models represent the link between hillslope and channel processes, identifying scaling relationships and limitations that affect how these widely-used models should be interpreted.

Publications

Litwin, D. G., Malatesta, L. C., & Sklar, L. S. (2025). Hillslope diffusion and channel steepness in landscape evolution models. Earth Surface Dynamics, 13(2), 277–293. https://doi.org/10.5194/esurf-13-277-2025

Permafrost thaw and groundwater

I'm developing models that couple shallow groundwater flow to the thermal dynamics of permafrost thaw, to understand how subsurface properties shape the emergence of melt patterns in Arctic and high-latitude landscapes.

Publications

Add citation here once this work is published.

Groundwater, slope creep, and failure

I'm working on how groundwater dynamics influence soil creep and slope failure under extreme precipitation — a growing hazard concern as storm intensity shifts with climate change.

Publications

Add citation here once this work is published.

Groundwater and tectonic geomorphology

In karst landscapes, groundwater can flow efficiently across topographic divides rather than following slope, with consequences for how landscapes respond to tectonics and baselevel change. I use numerical models to study these dynamics in places like the karstified plateau between the Rhine and Danube watersheds.

Publications

Add citation here once this work is published.

Hydrology and Landscape Dynamics Lab · Department of Earth and Environmental Science, Temple University · david.litwin@temple.edu
